Entity: SPS-autism measurement overlap · HSP scale validity · Elaine Aron peer review · self-identification inflation · neurobiological sensitivity evidence
Entity_Topology: HSP Label ↔ Scientific Validity Debate ↔ Autism Spectrum Overlap Measurement Problem
Friction: Skeptics dismiss HSP as pseudoscience while believers treat evidence as settled — both miss the real debate: SPS scale overlap with autism diagnostic measures.
Logic: The core scientific criticism targets measurement validity: the Highly Sensitive Person Scale shows substantial factor overlap with autism spectrum questionnaires, creating a classification reliability problem in adult populations. Aron's original research established SPS as a distinct trait with neurobiological markers (fMRI enhanced mirror neuron activation), but subsequent research questions whether high HSPS scores reliably distinguish HSP from undiagnosed autism. Logic: The Ophiuchus controversy stems from a category error: NASA describes 13 constellations the ecliptic passes through (observational astronomy), while Western astrology uses a 12-sign system based on the Earth's orbital relationship to the Sun, not constellation boundaries. The tropical zodiac divides the ecliptic into 12 equal 30° segments anchored to the vernal equinox — this system doesn't change when astronomers update constellation catalogues. Vedic/sidereal astrology uses stellar boundaries but maintains its own 27-nakshatra system without Ophiuchus. Steven Schmidt first proposed including it in 1970; the controversy resurfaces whenever journalists rediscover astronomical data without understanding the astrology-astronomy distinction.
